---
title: "Global Sensitivity Analysis for Pharmacometric Models"
author:
  - Narsimhan Sriram
  - Vijay Ivaturi
execute:
  cache: true
  error: false
julia: 
  exeflags: ["+Pumas@2.7.1"]
format:
  html:
    include-in-header:
      text: |
        <style>
          .header-container {
            width: 100%;
          }
          .logo {
            display: block;
            width: 150px;
            margin-left: -2.5%;
          }
        </style>
        <link rel = "shortcut icon" href = "https://pumas.ai/favicon.ico" />
    include-before-body:
      text: |
        <div class="header-container">
          <img src="https://pumas-assets.s3.amazonaws.com/CompanyLogos/PumasAI/RGB/PNG/Pumas+AI+Primary%404x.png" alt="Pumas Logo" class="logo">
        </div>
    self-contained-math: true
    anchor-sections: true
    theme: default
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 4
    toc-expand: 2
    toc-location: left
    toc-title: Contents
    number-sections: true
    code-summary: Show/Hide Code
embed-resources: true
fig-format: svg
fig-width: 8
fig-height: 6
bibliography: references.bib
csl: https://raw.githubusercontent.com/citation-style-language/styles/master/apa.csl
---

:::{.callout-note}
## Tumor Burden Tutorial Series

This is **Tutorial 3 of 6** in the Tumor Burden series.

| # | Tutorial | Status |
|---|----------|--------|
| 1 | [Model Introduction](tb_01_model_introduction_tutorial.qmd) | Complete |
| 2 | [Copula Vpop Generation](tb_02_copula_vpop_tutorial.qmd) | Complete |
| 3 | **Global Sensitivity Analysis (current)** | You are here |
| 4 | [Structural Identifiability](tb_04_structural_identifiability_tutorial.qmd) | |
| 5 | [MILP Calibration](tb_05_milp_calibration_tutorial.qmd) | |
| 6 | [VCT Simulation](tb_06_vct_simulation_tutorial.qmd) | |
:::

## Learning Outcomes

Global Sensitivity Analysis (GSA) is a powerful technique for understanding how model outputs depend on input parameters. In the context of In Silico Clinical Trials (ISCTs), GSA helps identify which parameters most influence treatment outcomes [@cortes2025].

In this tutorial, you will learn:

- What global sensitivity analysis is and why it differs from local sensitivity
- The difference between first-order and total-order sensitivity indices
- How to run GSA using Sobol and eFAST methods in Pumas
- How to interpret and visualize sensitivity analysis results
- How to use GSA to guide model development and ISCT design

:::{.callout-tip}
## What We'll Discover

By the end of this tutorial, we'll find that for the tumor burden model:

- **The sensitive fraction (f)** dominates at all timepoints, but especially at week 18 (Sₜ ≈ 0.84)
- **The death rate (k)** — despite controlling treatment efficacy — has minimal impact on *final* outcomes (Sₜ ≈ 0.07)
- **Parameter interactions are minimal** — the model behaves nearly additively

But here's the key insight: when we compare GSA across different timepoints, we'll discover that **k's influence is 5× stronger at week 4 (Sₜ ≈ 0.37) than at week 18**. Meanwhile, **g emerges from negligible (0.01) to influential (0.12)** as resistant cells drive late-stage tumor growth. The parameters that matter depend entirely on when you measure — a critical lesson for clinical trial design!
:::

## Prerequisites

This tutorial uses the Tumor Burden Model from [Tutorial 1](tb_01_model_introduction_tutorial.qmd). The model describes tumor dynamics with parameters:

- **f** (sensitive fraction): What fraction of tumor cells respond to treatment
- **g** (growth rate): How fast resistant cells proliferate
- **k** (death rate): How fast sensitive cells die under treatment

For model details, see Tutorial 1.

## Background

### What is Sensitivity Analysis?

**Sensitivity analysis** quantifies how variation in model outputs can be attributed to variation in model inputs. It answers two fundamental questions:

1. **Which parameters most affect the output?** — Identify the key drivers of model behavior
2. **Do parameters interact?** — Understand if combined parameter effects differ from individual effects

:::{.callout-note}
## Local vs Global Sensitivity Analysis

| Aspect | Local Sensitivity | Global Sensitivity |
|:-------|:------------------|:-------------------|
| **Approach** | Perturb one parameter slightly around a base value | Explore entire parameter space simultaneously |
| **Information** | Derivative (slope) at one point | Variance decomposition across full range |
| **Interactions** | Cannot detect | Explicitly quantified |
| **Computational cost** | Low (n evaluations) | High (thousands of evaluations) |
| **Robustness** | Depends on base point | Comprehensive |

**Local sensitivity** tells you what happens if you slightly change one parameter while holding others fixed. **Global sensitivity** tells you what happens across the entire plausible parameter space.
:::

### Why GSA in the ISCT Workflow?

In the ISCT workflow [@cortes2025], GSA serves several important purposes:

1. **Model understanding:** Identify which biological mechanisms drive treatment response
2. **Parameter prioritization:** Focus estimation efforts on influential parameters
3. **Experimental design:** Guide what to measure in clinical studies
4. **Model simplification:** Identify parameters that can be fixed without affecting predictions
5. **Uncertainty propagation:** Understand which parameter uncertainties matter most

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
    A["Build Model"] --> B["Run GSA"]
    B --> C{"Parameter\nInfluential?"}
    C -->|Yes| D["Estimate\nfrom data"]
    C -->|No| E["Fix to\nliterature value"]
    D --> F["Run ISCT"]
    E --> F

    style B fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100
    style C fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1565c0
```

### Variance-Based Sensitivity Indices

The most common GSA approach is **variance-based sensitivity analysis** [@sobol2001]. It decomposes the total output variance into contributions from each input parameter.

#### First-Order Sensitivity Index (S₁)

The **first-order index** measures the direct (main) effect of a single parameter:

$$S_i = \frac{V[\mathbb{E}[Y|X_i]]}{V[Y]}$$

**Interpretation:** The fraction of output variance explained by varying parameter $X_i$ alone, averaging over all other parameters.

- $S_i = 0$: Parameter has no direct effect
- $S_i = 1$: Parameter alone explains all output variance

#### Total-Order Sensitivity Index (Sₜ)

The **total-order index** measures the total effect including all interactions [@saltelli2010]:

$$S_{Ti} = 1 - \frac{V[\mathbb{E}[Y|X_{\sim i}]]}{V[Y]}$$

where $X_{\sim i}$ represents all parameters except $X_i$.

**Interpretation:** The fraction of output variance that would remain unexplained if we fixed parameter $X_i$.

:::{.callout-tip}
## Key Insight: Detecting Interactions

The difference between total-order and first-order indices reveals **parameter interactions**:

$$\text{Interaction effect} = S_{Ti} - S_i$$

| Situation | Meaning |
|:----------|:--------|
| $S_{Ti} \approx S_i$ | Parameter acts independently |
| $S_{Ti} > S_i$ | Parameter has important interactions with others |
| $\sum S_i \approx 1$ | Model is approximately additive |
| $\sum S_i < 1$ | Significant interactions present |
:::

### GSA Methods: Sobol vs eFAST

Two popular methods for computing sensitivity indices:

| Method | Full Name | Approach | Samples Required | Reference |
|:-------|:----------|:---------|:-----------------|:----------|
| **Sobol** | Sobol indices | Monte Carlo sampling | N(2k+2) | @sobol2001 |
| **eFAST** | Extended Fourier Amplitude Sensitivity Test | Fourier analysis | ~500-1000 | @saltelli1999 |

**Practical guidance:**

- **Sobol:** More accurate, especially for total-order indices. Use for final analysis.
- **eFAST:** Faster, good for initial exploration. Use for screening.

## Libraries

```{julia}
#| output: false
using Pumas
using GlobalSensitivity    # GSA methods (Sobol, eFAST)
using DataFrames
using AlgebraOfGraphics
using CairoMakie
using SummaryTables        # Nice table rendering
using Statistics
using LinearAlgebra: Diagonal
using Random
```

**What each package does:**

- `Pumas`: Pharmacometric modeling with `Pumas.gsa()` for sensitivity analysis
- `GlobalSensitivity`: Provides Sobol and eFAST methods (Sobol, eFAST)
- `DataFrames`: Organize and manipulate results
- `AlgebraOfGraphics` + `CairoMakie`: Publication-quality visualizations
- `SummaryTables`: Clean rendering of DataFrames in documents

## Part 1: Model Setup for GSA

### Tumor Burden Model with GSA Endpoints

For GSA, we need to define **scalar output endpoints** that summarize the model behavior. We do this using the `@observed` block:

```{julia}
tumor_model_gsa = @model begin
    @metadata begin
        desc = "Tumor Burden Model for GSA"
        timeu = u"d"
    end

    @param begin
        # Typical values (population parameters)
        tvf ∈ RealDomain(lower = 0.0, upper = 1.0, init = 0.27)
        tvg ∈ RealDomain(lower = 0.0, init = 0.0013)
        tvk ∈ RealDomain(lower = 0.0, init = 0.0091)

        # Random effect variance (will be fixed to tiny values for GSA)
        Ω ∈ PDiagDomain(init = [1e-10, 1e-10, 1e-10])
        σ ∈ RealDomain(lower = 0.0, init = 1e-10)
    end

    @random begin
        η ~ MvNormal(Ω)
    end

    @covariates treatment

    @pre begin
        # For GSA, we effectively use tvf, tvg, tvk directly
        # because η is negligible (Ω is tiny)
        # f uses logit-normal: logit(f) = logit(tvf) + η
        f = logistic(logit(tvf) + η[1])
        g = tvg * exp(η[2])
        k = tvk * exp(η[3])
        k_eff = treatment * k
    end

    @init begin
        N_sens = f
        N_res = 1.0 - f
    end

    @dynamics begin
        N_sens' = -k_eff * N_sens
        N_res' = g * N_res
    end

    @derived begin
        Nt = @. N_sens + N_res
        tumor_size ~ @. Normal(Nt, σ)
    end

    @observed begin
        # Scalar endpoints for GSA - clinically meaningful outputs
        # Tumor size at end of treatment (week 18 = day 126)
        final_tumor = last(Nt)
    end
end
```

:::{.callout-note}
## Understanding the @observed Block

The `@observed` block defines **scalar outputs** for GSA:

1. **Scalar values** (single number per simulation) - required for sensitivity analysis
2. **Derived from the time course** using functions like `last()`, indexing, or summary statistics
3. **Not used for fitting** (unlike `@derived`)

For the tumor model:

- `final_tumor`: Tumor size at the last observation time (treatment endpoint)

Common scalar endpoints for GSA:

- `last(variable)` - value at final timepoint
- `variable[index]` - value at specific timepoint
- `maximum(variable)` - peak value
- `minimum(variable)` - nadir value
:::

## Part 2: Running GSA

### Create a Subject for Simulation

GSA requires a "subject" to provide time points and covariates:

```{julia}
# Define observation times (18 weeks of treatment)
observation_times = 0:7:126  # Weekly for 18 weeks

# Create a subject (treatment arm)
subject = Subject(
    id = 1,
    covariates = (treatment = 1,),  # Treatment arm (drug active)
    time = collect(observation_times)
)

println("Subject created:")
println("  Observation times: $(collect(observation_times))")
println("  Treatment: active (k_eff = k)")
```

### Define Parameter Ranges

GSA explores parameters within specified bounds. These should cover the plausible physiological range:

```{julia}
# Lower bounds for typical values
p_range_low = (
    tvf = 0.05,    # At least 5% sensitive
    tvg = 0.0005,  # Minimum growth rate
    tvk = 0.002    # Minimum death rate
)

# Upper bounds for typical values
p_range_high = (
    tvf = 0.95,   # At most 95% sensitive
    tvg = 0.005,  # Maximum growth rate
    tvk = 0.05    # Maximum death rate
)

println("Parameter ranges for GSA:")
for key in keys(p_range_low)
    println("  $key: [$(p_range_low[key]), $(p_range_high[key])]")
end
```

### Base Parameters and Constant Coefficients

We need base parameter values and must fix the random effect variance to make simulations deterministic:

```{julia}
# Base parameters (center of ranges)
base_params = (
    tvf = 0.27,
    tvg = 0.0013,
    tvk = 0.0091,
    Ω = Diagonal([1e-10, 1e-10, 1e-10]),  # Tiny variance → deterministic
    σ = 1e-10                               # No residual noise
)

# Fix Ω and σ so they are NOT varied by GSA
# constantcoef takes a TUPLE OF SYMBOLS (parameter names to hold constant)
# The actual values come from base_params
constant_coef = (:Ω, :σ)

println("Constant coefficients (not varied):")
println("  Ω: diagonal with 1e-10 (effectively zero)")
println("  σ: 1e-10 (no observation noise)")
```

:::{.callout-important}
## Why Fix Ω and σ?

For GSA, we want to understand how **population-level parameters** (tvf, tvg, tvk) affect outputs. If we allowed Ω to be large:

1. Random effects η would vary between simulations
2. This would add noise unrelated to parameter sensitivity
3. Sensitivity indices would be contaminated

By specifying `constantcoef = (:Ω, :σ)` (a **tuple of symbols**), we tell GSA to hold these parameters constant at their `base_params` values. This gives deterministic simulations where only the parameters of interest vary.

**Note:** `constantcoef` expects a tuple of parameter names (symbols), not a NamedTuple with values. The actual values come from `base_params`.
:::

### Run eFAST (Fast Initial Analysis)

```{julia}
# Run eFAST sensitivity analysis
# eFAST is faster than Sobol, good for initial exploration
Random.seed!(123)

gsa_efast = Pumas.gsa(
    tumor_model_gsa,              # Model
    subject,                       # Subject with time points
    base_params,                   # Base parameter values
    eFAST(),                       # Method (from GlobalSensitivity)
    [:final_tumor],    # Outputs to analyze
    p_range_low,                   # Lower bounds
    p_range_high;                  # Upper bounds
    constantcoef = constant_coef,  # Parameters NOT to vary (tuple of symbols)
    samples = 500                  # Number of samples
)

println("eFAST analysis complete!")
println("Method: eFAST with 500 samples")
```

### Extract Results

```{julia}
# First-order indices
println("First-Order Sensitivity Indices (S₁):")
println("-" ^ 50)
for (output_idx, output) in enumerate([:final_tumor])
    println("\n$output:")
    row = gsa_efast.first_order[output_idx, :]
    for param in [:tvf, :tvg, :tvk]
        println("  $param: $(round(row[param], digits=4))")
    end
end

# Total-order indices
println("\n" * "=" ^ 50)
println("Total-Order Sensitivity Indices (Sₜ):")
println("-" ^ 50)
for (output_idx, output) in enumerate([:final_tumor])
    println("\n$output:")
    row = gsa_efast.total_order[output_idx, :]
    for param in [:tvf, :tvg, :tvk]
        println("  $param: $(round(row[param], digits=4))")
    end
end
```

### Run Sobol (More Accurate)

For publication-quality results, use the Sobol method:

```{julia}
Random.seed!(456)

gsa_sobol = Pumas.gsa(
    tumor_model_gsa,
    subject,
    base_params,
    Sobol(nboot = 100),            # Method with bootstrap for confidence intervals
    [:final_tumor],
    p_range_low,
    p_range_high;
    constantcoef = constant_coef,  # Parameters NOT to vary (tuple of symbols)
    samples = 1000                 # Number of samples
)

println("Sobol analysis complete!")
println("Method: Sobol with 1000 samples and 100 bootstrap replicates")
```

## Part 3: Interpreting Results

### Compile Results into DataFrame

```{julia}
# Helper function to extract GSA results
function extract_gsa_results(gsa_result, param_names, output_names)
    rows = NamedTuple[]

    for (i, output) in enumerate(output_names)
        fo_row = gsa_result.first_order[i, :]
        to_row = gsa_result.total_order[i, :]

        for param in param_names
            push!(rows, (
                output = output,
                parameter = param,
                first_order = round(fo_row[param], digits=4),
                total_order = round(to_row[param], digits=4),
                interaction = round(to_row[param] - fo_row[param], digits=4)
            ))
        end
    end

    return DataFrame(rows)
end

# Extract results
param_names = [:tvf, :tvg, :tvk]
output_names = [:final_tumor]

results_df = extract_gsa_results(gsa_sobol, param_names, output_names)
simple_table(results_df)
```

### Rank Parameters by Importance

```{julia}
# For each output, rank parameters by total-order index
ranked_results = DataFrame()

for output in output_names
    output_data = filter(row -> row.output == output, results_df)
    sorted_data = sort(output_data, :total_order, rev=true)
    sorted_data.rank = 1:nrow(sorted_data)
    append!(ranked_results, sorted_data)
end

println("Parameters ranked by importance:")
simple_table(ranked_results)
```

### Identify Influential Parameters

```{julia}
# Parameters with total-order index ≥ 0.1 are considered influential
threshold = 0.1

influential = filter(row -> row.total_order >= threshold, results_df)
influential_params = unique(influential.parameter)

println("Influential parameters (Sₜ ≥ $threshold):")
for p in influential_params
    println("  - $p")
end

println("\nFor final_tumor:")
final_data = filter(row -> row.output == :final_tumor, ranked_results)
for row in eachrow(final_data)
    status = row.total_order >= threshold ? "INFLUENTIAL" : "minor"
    println("  $(row.rank). $(row.parameter): Sₜ = $(row.total_order) [$status]")
end
```

:::{.callout-note}
## Interpretation of Results

Looking at the **final_tumor** output:

1. **tvf (sensitive fraction)** has the highest total-order index (~0.84) — the dominant driver of final tumor size
2. **tvg (growth rate)** is second (~0.12) — influences resistant cell regrowth
3. **tvk (death rate)** is minor (~0.07) — less influential for the final outcome

**Biological interpretation:**

- The fraction of treatment-sensitive cells (f) is the primary determinant of long-term outcome
- Patients with high f have more cells killed by treatment → smaller final tumors
- Growth rate (g) matters because resistant cells eventually dominate the tumor
- Death rate (k) has less impact on final size because it only affects the sensitive population, which is eventually eliminated regardless of k

**Why is f dominant?**

The model: $N(t) = N_0 \cdot f \cdot e^{-kt} + N_0 \cdot (1-f) \cdot e^{gt}$

At late times, the first term (sensitive cells) → 0 regardless of k, so final tumor ≈ $N_0 \cdot (1-f) \cdot e^{gT}$. This depends strongly on f and g, but not k.
:::

## Part 4: Visualization

### Bar Chart: First-Order vs Total-Order

```{julia}
#| fig-cap: "Sensitivity indices for final tumor size. Dark bars show first-order (direct) effects; light extensions show interaction contributions. The red dashed line indicates the importance threshold (Sₜ > 0.1)."

# Filter for final_tumor and reshape for AoG
final_data = filter(row -> row.output == :final_tumor, results_df)

# Sort by total order for better visualization
final_data = sort(final_data, :total_order, rev = true)

# Create long-form DataFrame for stacked bar visualization
bar_df = vcat(
    DataFrame(
        parameter = string.(final_data.parameter),
        value = final_data.first_order,
        index_type = fill("First-order (S₁)", nrow(final_data))
    ),
    DataFrame(
        parameter = string.(final_data.parameter),
        value = final_data.interaction,
        index_type = fill("Interaction (Sₜ - S₁)", nrow(final_data))
    )
)

# Create plot with AoG - use sorter to preserve order
plt = data(bar_df) *
    mapping( :parameter => sorter(string.(final_data.parameter)),:value,
            color = :index_type => "Index Type", dodge = :index_type) *
    visual(BarPlot)
plt_hlines = mapping([0.1]) * visual(HLines, color = :red, linestyle = :dash, linewidth = 2); 
plt_text = mapping(2.8, 0.12) * visual(Makie.Text, text = "Importance\nThreshold = 0.1", color = :red, fontsize = 10)   
draw(plt + plt_hlines + plt_text, 
    axis = (title = "Sensitivity Analysis: Final Tumor Size",
            xlabel = "Parameter",
            ylabel = "Sensitivity Index",
            xticks = (1:3, ["f (sensitive fraction)", "g (growth rate)", "k (death rate)"]),
            limits = (nothing, nothing, 0.0, 1.0)
            ),
    legend = (position = :bottom, framevisible = false, nbanks = 1, titleposition = :left),
    figure = (size = (800, 600),)
)
```

:::{.callout-note}
## Interpreting the Sensitivity Bar Chart

**How to read this plot:**

- **Bar length** = total sensitivity (Sₜ) of each parameter
- **Dark blue** = first-order effect (S₁) — direct influence of the parameter alone
- **Light blue** = interaction contribution (Sₜ - S₁) — influence through parameter interactions

**Key findings:**

1. **f (sensitive fraction)** has the highest sensitivity (Sₜ ≈ 0.85), meaning it explains ~85% of the variance in final tumor size. This makes biological sense: the fraction of treatment-sensitive cells directly determines how much the tumor can shrink.

2. **g (growth rate)** has moderate sensitivity (Sₜ ≈ 0.15). The resistant cell growth rate matters, but less than the sensitive fraction.

3. **k (death rate)** has very low sensitivity (Sₜ < 0.1). This is because at the final timepoint (126 days), most sensitive cells have already died regardless of the exact death rate.

**The red dashed line** marks Sₜ = 0.1, a common threshold for identifying "influential" parameters. Parameters above this line warrant careful estimation; those below can potentially be fixed to literature values.
:::

### Ranked Parameter Importance

```{julia}
#| fig-cap: "Parameters ranked by total-order sensitivity index for final tumor size."

# Sort parameters by total-order index (descending) and create DataFrame for AoG
sorted_data = sort(results_df, :total_order, rev = true)

# Create DataFrame with rank for ordering
rank_df = DataFrame(
    parameter = string.(sorted_data.parameter),
    total_order = sorted_data.total_order,
    rank = 1:nrow(sorted_data)
)
# Assign colors based on rank (most important = firebrick)
rank_df.color_group = ["Rank $i" for i in rank_df.rank]

# Create plot with AoG - use sorter to control order
plt = data(rank_df) *
    mapping(:parameter => sorter(rank_df.parameter) => "Parameter",
            :total_order => "Total-Order Sensitivity Index (Sₜ)",
            color = :color_group => "") *
    visual(BarPlot)
plt_hlines = mapping([0.1]) * visual(HLines, color = :red, linestyle = :dash, linewidth = 2);   
draw(plt + plt_hlines,
    axis = (title = "Parameter Importance Ranking for Final Tumor Size",
            xlabel = "Parameter",
            ylabel = "Total Order Sensitivity Index",
            xticks = (1:3, ["f (sensitive fraction)", "g (growth rate)", "k (death rate)"]),
            limits = (nothing, nothing, 0.0, 1.0)
            ),
    legend = (position = :bottom, framevisible = false, nbanks = 1, titleposition = :left),
    figure = (size = (800, 600),)
)
```

### Comparison: First-Order vs Total-Order

```{julia}
#| fig-cap: "Scatter plot comparing first-order and total-order indices. Points above the diagonal indicate parameters with significant interactions."

# Create DataFrame for AoG scatter plot
scatter_df = DataFrame(
    first_order = results_df.first_order,
    total_order = results_df.total_order,
    parameter = [string(p)[3:end] for p in results_df.parameter],  # Remove "tv" prefix
    param_label = ["f (sensitive fraction)", "g (growth rate)", "k (death rate)"]
)

# Create scatter plot with AoG
plt = data(scatter_df) *
    mapping(:first_order => "First-Order Index (S₁)",
            :total_order => "Total-Order Index (Sₜ)",
            color = :param_label => "Parameter") *
    visual(Scatter, markersize = 20)
plt_abline = mapping([0], [1]) * visual(ABLines)
plt_text = mapping(0.6, 0.55) * visual(Makie.Text, text = "No interactions", color = :gray, fontsize = 10, rotation = atan(1))        
draw(plt + plt_abline + plt_text,
    axis = (title = "Detecting Parameter Interactions",
             xlabel = "First-Order Sensitivity Index (S₁)",
             ylabel = "Total-Order Sensitivity Index (Sₜ)",
             limits = (0, 1, 0, 1),
             aspect = 1
            ),
    legend = (position = :bottom, framevisible = false, nbanks = 1, titleposition = :left),
    figure = (size = (650, 500),)
)
```

## Part 5: GSA Across Timepoints

### Why Timepoint Matters

Our analysis used final tumor size (week 18) as the endpoint. But what if we chose a different timepoint? This section demonstrates how **parameter importance changes depending on when you measure the outcome**.

### Model with Multiple Timepoint Outputs

```{julia}
# Define a model that outputs tumor size at specific timepoints
tumor_model_timepoints = @model begin
    @metadata begin
        desc = "Tumor Burden Model - Multiple Timepoints"
        timeu = u"d"
    end

    @param begin
        tvf ∈ RealDomain(lower = 0.0, upper = 1.0, init = 0.27)
        tvg ∈ RealDomain(lower = 0.0, init = 0.0013)
        tvk ∈ RealDomain(lower = 0.0, init = 0.0091)
        Ω ∈ PDiagDomain(init = [1e-10, 1e-10, 1e-10])
        σ ∈ RealDomain(lower = 0.0, init = 1e-10)
    end

    @random begin
        η ~ MvNormal(Ω)
    end

    @covariates treatment

    @pre begin
        # f uses logit-normal: logit(f) = logit(tvf) + η
        f = logistic(logit(tvf) + η[1])
        g = tvg * exp(η[2])
        k = tvk * exp(η[3])
        k_eff = treatment * k
    end

    @init begin
        N_sens = f
        N_res = 1.0 - f
    end

    @dynamics begin
        N_sens' = -k_eff * N_sens
        N_res' = g * N_res
    end

    @derived begin
        Nt = @. N_sens + N_res
        tumor_size ~ @. Normal(Nt, σ)
    end

    @observed begin
        # Tumor size at different timepoints
        # Week 4 = day 28, Week 8 = day 56, Week 18 = day 126
        tumor_week4 = Nt[5]     # Index 5 corresponds to day 28 (weekly observations starting at day 0)
        tumor_week8 = Nt[9]     # Index 9 corresponds to day 56
        tumor_week18 = Nt[19]   # Index 19 corresponds to day 126
    end
end

println("Model defined with outputs at weeks 4, 8, and 18")
```

### Run Comparative GSA

```{julia}
# Create subject with weekly observations for 18 weeks
obs_times_weekly = 0:7:126  # Days 0, 7, 14, ..., 126

subject_timepoints = Subject(
    id = 1,
    covariates = (treatment = 1,),
    time = collect(obs_times_weekly)
)

# Run Sobol GSA for all three timepoints
Random.seed!(789)

gsa_timepoints = Pumas.gsa(
    tumor_model_timepoints,
    subject_timepoints,
    base_params,
    Sobol(nboot = 100),
    [:tumor_week4, :tumor_week8, :tumor_week18],
    p_range_low,
    p_range_high;
    constantcoef = constant_coef,
    samples = 1000
)

println("Comparative GSA complete!")
```

### Extract and Compare Results

```{julia}
# Extract results for each timepoint
timepoint_results = DataFrame()

timepoints = [:tumor_week4, :tumor_week8, :tumor_week18]
timepoint_labels = ["Week 4", "Week 8", "Week 18"]

for (i, (tp, label)) in enumerate(zip(timepoints, timepoint_labels))
    fo_row = gsa_timepoints.first_order[i, :]
    to_row = gsa_timepoints.total_order[i, :]

    for param in [:tvf, :tvg, :tvk]
        push!(timepoint_results, (
            timepoint = label,
            parameter = param,
            first_order = round(fo_row[param], digits=4),
            total_order = round(to_row[param], digits=4)
        ))
    end
end

# Pivot for easier comparison
println("Sensitivity Indices by Timepoint:")
println("=" ^ 60)

for tp in timepoint_labels
    println("\n$tp:")
    tp_data = filter(row -> row.timepoint == tp, timepoint_results)
    tp_sorted = sort(tp_data, :total_order, rev=true)
    for row in eachrow(tp_sorted)
        status = row.total_order >= 0.1 ? "INFLUENTIAL" : "minor"
        println("  $(row.parameter): Sₜ = $(row.total_order) [$status]")
    end
end
```

### Visualize Parameter Importance Over Time

```{julia}
#| fig-cap: "How parameter importance changes across treatment timepoints. Early: k matters for active killing. Late: f dominates as resistant cells determine outcome."

# Create DataFrame for AoG with clean parameter names
timepoint_plot_df = DataFrame(
    timepoint = timepoint_results.timepoint,
    parameter = [string(p)[3:end] for p in timepoint_results.parameter],  # Remove "tv" prefix
    total_order = timepoint_results.total_order
)

# Create faceted bar plot with AoG
plt = data(timepoint_plot_df) *
    mapping(:parameter => sorter(["f", "g", "k"]) => "Parameter",
            :total_order => "Total-Order Sensitivity Index (Sₜ)",
            color = :parameter => "Parameter",
            layout = :timepoint => sorter(["Week 4", "Week 8", "Week 18"])) *
    visual(BarPlot)
draw(plt,
    axis = (xlabel = "Parameter",
            ylabel = "Total-Order Sensitivity Index (Sₜ)",
            xticks = (1:3, ["f", "g", "k"]),
            limits = (nothing, nothing, 0.0, 1.0)
            ),
    legend = (position = :bottom, framevisible = false, nbanks = 1, titleposition = :left),
    figure = (size = (900, 800), title = "Parameter Sensitivity Across Timepoints",)
)
```

### Summary Table: Parameter Rankings Over Time

```{julia}
# Create summary comparison table by extracting values for each parameter
function get_st(param, timepoint)
    row = filter(r -> r.timepoint == timepoint && r.parameter == param, timepoint_results)
    return round(row[1, :total_order], digits=2)
end

summary_comparison = DataFrame(
    Parameter = ["f (sensitive fraction)", "g (growth rate)", "k (death rate)"],
    Week_4 = [get_st(:tvf, "Week 4"), get_st(:tvg, "Week 4"), get_st(:tvk, "Week 4")],
    Week_8 = [get_st(:tvf, "Week 8"), get_st(:tvg, "Week 8"), get_st(:tvk, "Week 8")],
    Week_18 = [get_st(:tvf, "Week 18"), get_st(:tvg, "Week 18"), get_st(:tvk, "Week 18")]
)

simple_table(summary_comparison)
```

:::{.callout-important}
## Key Insight: Endpoint Choice Matters!

This comparative analysis demonstrates a critical point: **parameter rankings shift depending on when you measure**.

| If Your Question Is... | Most Sensitive To | Why |
|:-----------------------|:------------------|:----|
| "How effective is treatment at killing tumor cells?" | f (always), k (early) | k influence peaks at week 4 (Sₜ=0.37) during active killing |
| "What is the long-term prognosis?" | f (dominant), g (emerging) | g grows from 0.01 → 0.12 as regrowth drives outcomes |
| "How fast will the tumor regrow?" | g (late timepoints) | g only becomes influential (Sₜ > 0.1) after week 8 |

**Practical implications:**

1. **For drug development (efficacy):** Measure tumor response at week 4-8 to capture k's influence (Sₜ = 0.24-0.37)
2. **For survival prediction:** f dominates at all timepoints, but g becomes relevant late
3. **For clinical trial design:** k appears "unimportant" at week 18 (Sₜ = 0.07) but is clearly influential at week 4 (Sₜ = 0.37) — choosing only late endpoints would miss this

**The key lesson:** f is always dominant in this model, but k's influence is **5× stronger at week 4 than week 18** (0.37 vs 0.07). A GSA run only at the final timepoint would incorrectly suggest k doesn't matter for treatment efficacy.
:::

## Part 6: Practical Workflow

### Complete GSA Function

Here's a reusable function for running GSA:

```{julia}
"""
    run_tumor_gsa(;method=:efast, n_samples=500, observation_times=0:7:126)

Run GSA on the tumor burden model.

# Arguments
- `method`: :efast or :sobol
- `n_samples`: Number of samples (500-1000 for eFAST, N for Sobol)
- `observation_times`: Time points for simulation

# Returns
DataFrame with sensitivity indices
"""
function run_tumor_gsa(;
    method::Symbol = :efast,
    n_samples::Int = 500,
    observation_times = 0:7:126
)
    # Create subject
    subject = Subject(
        id = 1,
        covariates = (treatment = 1,),
        time = collect(observation_times)
    )

    # Parameters
    base_params = (
        tvf = 0.27, tvg = 0.0013, tvk = 0.0091,
        Ω = Diagonal([1e-10, 1e-10, 1e-10]),
        σ = 1e-10
    )

    p_low = (tvf = 0.05, tvg = 0.0005, tvk = 0.002)
    p_high = (tvf = 0.95, tvg = 0.005, tvk = 0.05)
    const_coef = (:Ω, :σ)  # Tuple of symbols - parameters NOT to vary

    # Run GSA
    # Sobol with bootstrap for confidence intervals; eFAST for quick screening
    gsa_method = method == :sobol ? Sobol(nboot = 100) : eFAST()

    result = Pumas.gsa(
        tumor_model_gsa, subject, base_params, gsa_method,
        [:final_tumor], p_low, p_high;
        constantcoef = const_coef,
        samples = n_samples
    )

    # Extract results
    return extract_gsa_results(result, [:tvf, :tvg, :tvk], [:final_tumor])
end
```

### Example Usage

```{julia}
# Quick screening with eFAST
quick_results = run_tumor_gsa(method = :efast, n_samples = 300)

# Identify influential parameters
influential = filter(row -> row.total_order >= 0.1, quick_results)
println("Influential parameters from quick screening:")
for row in eachrow(influential)
    println("  $(row.parameter) for $(row.output): Sₜ = $(row.total_order)")
end
```

## Practical Recommendations

:::{.callout-tip}
## 1. Choosing Sample Size

| Method | Recommended Samples | Use Case |
|:-------|:--------------------|:---------|
| eFAST | 300-500 | Quick screening, exploration |
| Sobol | 500-2000 | Final analysis, publication |

For more accurate Sobol indices, use `nboot = 100` for bootstrap confidence intervals.
:::

:::{.callout-tip}
## 2. Selecting Parameter Ranges

- Cover the **physiologically plausible** range
- Use **literature values** for bounds when available
- For population parameters, use **typical ± 3×CV** as a rule of thumb
- Avoid ranges where the model becomes unstable
:::

:::{.callout-tip}
## 3. Using GSA to Guide Model Development

| GSA Finding | Action |
|:------------|:-------|
| Sₜ < 0.01 | Parameter can be fixed to any reasonable value |
| 0.01 < Sₜ < 0.1 | Parameter can be fixed to literature value |
| Sₜ > 0.1 | Parameter should be estimated from data |
| Sₜ - S₁ > 0.1 | Important interactions; consider interaction terms |
:::

:::{.callout-warning}
## 4. Common Pitfalls

1. **Forgetting to fix Ω:** Random effects add noise that contaminates sensitivity indices. Use `constantcoef = (:Ω, :σ)` to hold them constant.
2. **Wrong constantcoef type:** `constantcoef` expects a tuple of symbols like `(:Ω, :σ)`, NOT a NamedTuple with values.
3. **Too narrow parameter ranges:** May miss important nonlinear effects
4. **Too few samples:** Indices may be unstable; increase N if results vary between runs
5. **Ignoring outputs:** Different outputs may have different influential parameters
:::

## Summary

### Results from This Tutorial

**Final tumor size (Week 18):**

| Parameter | Sₜ | Classification | Action |
|:----------|:---|:---------------|:-------|
| **tvf** (sensitive fraction) | 0.844 | Dominant | Must estimate accurately |
| **tvg** (growth rate) | 0.121 | Influential | Should estimate from data |
| **tvk** (death rate) | 0.074 | Minor | Can fix to literature value |

**Key insight:** The fraction of treatment-sensitive cells (f) explains ~84% of the variance in final tumor size.

**Timepoint comparison reveals the full story:**

| Parameter | Week 4 | Week 8 | Week 18 | Trend |
|:----------|:-------|:-------|:--------|:------|
| **f** | 0.70 | 0.78 | 0.84 | Always dominant, increasing |
| **g** | 0.01 | 0.03 | 0.12 | Negligible → influential |
| **k** | 0.37 | 0.24 | 0.07 | Influential → minor |

### Key Takeaways

1. **GSA quantifies how outputs depend on inputs** across the entire parameter space, not just at a single point

2. **First-order indices (S₁)** measure direct effects; **total-order indices (Sₜ)** include interactions

3. **eFAST is faster**, good for screening; **Sobol is more accurate**, use for final analysis

4. **Fix Ω and σ** via `constantcoef = (:Ω, :σ)` (tuple of symbols) to ensure deterministic simulations

5. **Parameters with Sₜ < 0.1** can often be fixed to literature values without affecting predictions

6. **Endpoint choice critically affects results:** k dropped from Sₜ = 0.37 (week 4) to 0.07 (week 18)

### What's Next

In **Tutorial 4: Structural Identifiability**, we will:

- Learn whether parameters CAN be uniquely estimated from data
- Understand the difference between identifiable and non-identifiable parameters
- Discover that f is non-identifiable from total tumor measurements alone

### Quick Reference: Key Functions

| Function | Package | Purpose |
|:---------|:--------|:--------|
| `Pumas.gsa(model, subject, params, method, outputs, p_low, p_high; constantcoef, samples)` | Pumas | Run GSA |
| `Sobol(nboot = 100)` | GlobalSensitivity | Sobol method with bootstrap CI |
| `eFAST()` | GlobalSensitivity | eFAST method (faster screening) |
| `last(variable)` | Base Julia | Get value at final timepoint (for `@observed`) |

### References

::: {#refs}
:::
